steps.text: Verify that the sample integration time is sufficient to achieve the required signal-to-noise ratio.
Assign a unique file name to each spectrum.
Inspect the sample spectra immediately after the run to verify the gas matrix composition was close to the expected matrix composition.
Verify that the sampling and instrumental parameters were appropriate for the actual stack conditions.
Compare the pre- and post-test CTS spectra.
Alternate the measurement of the native and SA-elevated source gas so that each measurement of SA-elevated source gas is immediately preceded and followed by a measurement of native stack gas.
Introduce the SA gases in such a manner that the entire sampling system is challenged.

constraints.text: The peak absorbance in the pre- and post-test CTS must be ±5% of the mean value.
Spiking must be done dynamically accounting for the spike dilution of sample gas with the addition of the reference gas.
Pre and post-test spiking must consist of at least 3 replicates.

steps.text: Determine the absolute noise equivalent absorption (NEA) for an analytical region by flowing nitrogen or zero air through the gas sample cell.
Collect two background spectra in succession while the nitrogen or zero air is continuously flowing through the cell.
Calculate NEARMSCTS per the equation provided in section 10.3.2.
Obtain reference spectra for each analyte, interferant, surrogate, CTS, and tracer.

Report traceability and other pertinent information for each reference spectrum, for each compound, including temperature, pressure, concentration, cylinder source, spectral regions, and calibration fit equations.

Perform a direct injection of the compound of interest (NIST traceable and certified to ±5%) into the FTIR at a concentration lower than that found in the sample stream and within three times the method detection level.
Wait for the concentration of the compound to stabilize while directly injecting it into the FTIR.
Verify that the concentration as determined via the calibration curve is within 10% of the cylinder value.
Use calculated spectra, such as those from HITRAN or PNNL, at the lower end of the bracketing range, within three times the method detection level.
Record a set of CTS spectra.
Collect a set of the reference spectra at two or more concentrations in triplicate over the desired concentration range.
Collect a second set of CTS spectra.

preconditions.text: If commercially prepared, or other available reference libraries are used to quantify data
In the case where traceable reference spectra provided by the FTIR manufacturer do not bracket the concentration of a particular compound
If it is necessary for the tester to collect reference spectra prior to testing

constraints.text: The same averaging time must be used for NEA determination as will be used for actual sample collection.
The FTIR spectral resolution and line position, cell pathlength, temperature and pressure, and apodization function must be known and reported.
Resolution, line position, and apodization function used for collection of sample spectra must be the same as those of the reference spectra used for quantitation.
Reference spectra for each target compound must bracket the concentration of that compound in the sample stream.
Do not proceed with testing if the concentration as determined via the calibration curve is not within 10% of the cylinder value.
The top of the concentration range must be less than 10 times that of the bottom of the range.

steps.text: Seal the probe end of the system by capping or plugging the end of the sample probe.
Start sampling pumps and operate them until the pressure stabilizes.
Observe/measure the flow through the vacuum-side of the sampling system.
Seal the probe end of the system as close to the probe opening as possible by capping or plugging the end of the sample probe.
Operate all vacuum pumps.
Draw a vacuum on the sampling system and let the pressure on the system stabilize.
Turn off the sample pumps and seal the system under a vacuum of 250 mmHg greater than the source static pressure.
Record the absolute pressure and the system absolute temperature every 30 seconds for 5 minutes.
Observe the single beam instrument response in the frequency region below the detector cutoff (usually <400 cm-1), where the detector response is known to be zero.
Verify that the detector response is 'flat' and equal to zero in this region, or at least 100 times less than the peak signal in the entire spectrum.
Decrease the aperture or attenuate the IR beam, and repeat the linearity check until the detector response is linear.
Verify the gas cell pathlength of your instrument by following the procedure found in section 10.6.4 of this method.
Choose one of two options and perform the standard addition procedure listed in section 9.3 of this method.

preconditions.text: For bypass systems, where the sample flow rate through the vacuum side of the sample system is greater than the FTIR cell flow rate, the higher flow rate (bypass plus analyzer/FTIR flow rate) is used as the in-use flow rate when calculating acceptability of the leak level.
If the response is not linear

constraints.text: A flow of less than 0.5% of the system’s normal in-use flow rate is acceptable.
The leak rate must be equal to or less than 2.5 mmHg per minute.
